title: The Twenty-Third Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver
October 31, 2021 | Broderick Greer, Preacher

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Twenty-Third Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time, falling on All Hallows' Eve. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would likely include Habakkuk 1:1-4; 2:1-4 and Psalm 119:137-144 (Old Testament/Psalm), 2 Thessalonians 1:1-4, 11-12 (Epistle), and Luke 19:1-10 (the Zacchaeus story, Gospel). The season is Ordinary Time, the longest stretch of the Christian year running from Epiphany through Christ the King Sunday, with no major feast days or liturgical emphases.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the midst of its three-year cycle between General Conventions, with the next major gathering scheduled for July 2022 in Baltimore. The Diocese of Colorado, which oversees Saint John's Cathedral, was operating under Bishop Robert Wright (installed 2016). No major denominational controversies or bishop elections were underway nationally at this specific moment. The broader Anglican Communion was navigating ongoing tensions regarding LGBTQ inclusion and biblical interpretation, though these were persistent rather than acute in late October 2021.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical late autumn weather with temperatures in the 50s-60s Fahrenheit and scattered snow possible in higher elevations. The city had recently completed a contentious election cycle with municipal ballots being decided on November 2, 2021 (one day after this sermon), including races for mayor and multiple ballot measures on public safety and development. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, home to the Cathedral, was experiencing the ongoing economic impacts of the pandemic, with some storefronts still vacant and the neighborhood's nightlife and arts scene in partial recovery.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Biden administration was facing an approval rating decline as inflation concerns mounted and pandemic recovery remained uneven; the economy was the dominant domestic story. Congress was deadlocked over infrastructure spending and Build Back Better legislation, with negotiations ongoing. The NFL was in mid-season with the Denver Broncos struggling (they finished 7-10 in 2021); Major League Baseball's World Series was concluding (Atlanta Braves beat Houston Astros on November 2). The Kyle Rittenhouse trial was underway in Wisconsin and drawing significant national media attention ahead of its verdict on November 19, 2021.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Afghanistan remained a humanitarian crisis in the immediate aftermath of the August 2021 U.S. withdrawal, with Taliban governance, economic collapse, and refugee flows dominating international coverage. The UN Climate Change Conference (COP26) was scheduled to begin in Glasgow on November 1, 2021, just one day after this sermon, generating pre-conference media coverage about climate commitments and negotiations. The U.K. was navigating post-Brexit economic adjustments with supply chain disruptions and labor shortages affecting retail and logistics sectors.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American society was in a transitional, somewhat anxious period: pandemic restrictions were largely lifted, but the sense of normalcy remained fragile with new variants of COVID-19 still circulating and vaccination debates ongoing. Halloween (October 31) represented a return to pre-pandemic social rituals, with trick-or-treating and festivities resuming more fully than in 2020, carrying symbolic weight as a sign of recovery. There was broad cultural weariness with both the pandemic and polarization; the zeitgeist was one of exhaustion, economic anxiety, and fractured consensus—not yet the optimism of "return to normal," but not the acute crisis mode of 2020 either.
